sentences.text: What can I tell you?
It was just a very small function.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were married, in Heath-- it was at Heathfield Park Shul, which is in Willesden.
And we had a small tea party afterwards, just with immediate friends.
There weren't really any relatives, of course.
Oh, no.
That's not true.
I'm sorry.
I've just remembered.
I think my cousin, no I've made a mistake.
That was the second one, having had two marriages, I got a bit confused.
It was just a small wedding, with a few people, and we had a tea party afterwards.
That's really basically what it was.
My husband's three years-- three years older than I am.
And he also had a-- he had a very tough background as well.
He came-- he was born in Vienna.
And during the war, he was taken by the French Baroness of Rothschild to France.
She gave over one of her rather beautiful homes, called [NON-ENGLISH], which was a small castle, about 30 miles from Paris.
He spent the first few months there, until Hitler walked in.
And then it was dispersed.
And they were-- they had to just run for their lives.
And they went-- the youngsters went all over France.
They headed for the south of France.
And I believe as soon as he looked old enough, he joined the Marquis, and he came to England after the war, thinking that his mother was in England, which she was.
She came from Vienna.
She also had a most unhappy background.
Her husband was taken into one of the concentration camps.
I don't know which one.
But in those days it was well before the war, with her last few pennies she bought him out, and they let him out because he was dying.
And he died soon afterwards.
On the day that she buried him, she had to leave for England.
And she came on one of those permits.
I can't think of the official name of them, domestic permits.
She came, and she was a very ill lady.
And she spent a lot of her time in hospital, and very poor.
But anyway, they found each other after the war, and managed eventually to live in the same house, again in, just in a room.
You know, she had one room she shared.
He had another room.
And then when we married, he moved into [LAUGHS] my room.
Sorry?
What were you going to say?
Quite a few of us married young, I think, because we didn't have anybody.
We all got married quite young.
Not-- now, most youngsters seem to marry much later.
But I think we all married young, because we just had nobody.
So we got married at a very early age.
I think I was-- I was-- again, I was very adult in many ways, but very behind in-- I mean, I didn't know what family life meant, and so on.
And the marriage unfortunately didn't last.
I was married in '49, and I was divorced in '65.
I had one child from that marriage.
He came-- he came to England without a profession.
I think he was only 15 or 16, probably, or possibly 17 at the time.
And he-- somebody suggested, you should go into the diamond trade.
To get into the diamond trade was very, very difficult.
First of all, you had to be ultra-orthodox.
And he tried to get in, and he couldn't.
And then somebody said to him, wear a kippah next time you go.
And he managed to get in to the diamond trade.
The diamond trade was flourishing at the time.
But soon afterwards it became very erratic.
And there were times when he had no work.
That meant no money.
Not-- it's not again like now, if you didn't work, you didn't have any money.
So it became really bad.
And eventually it became so bad that he decided-- I think the factory closed down.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And through a distant-- very, very distant relative that I've mentioned before, we found another job-- he found another job.
It was something completely different.
But he made a go of it.
And through this job, he met a-- a young man from Israel who was working and living here.
And they started a business which flourished, and became a very good business.
Yes, I did, funnily enough.
When-- I had to get permission to get married.
Because I was underage.
So I had to go to Woburn House to get permission.
And they said to me, you are going to get 50 pounds, which was a fortune as far as I was concerned in 1949.
So I said, why?
Where do I get-- why will I get 50 pounds?
So, they told me that a man had died.
He must be a fantastic man, anonymous.
Anonymously, he wouldn't give his-- I never knew his name.
He had left a sum of money to be given to any refugee girl who was an orphan, 50 pounds at a time.
And I got the last 50 pounds.
So I was rich.
I had 50 pounds when I married.
That's all we had, more or less, between us.
But afterwards, you did, there was no--
No.
We had no financial help.
I did get, like every other refugee child, I got money from Germany.
I had 800 pounds, which was for loss of education.
And I got 1,000 pounds for the fact that I was an orphan.
Unfortunately, I never-- I claimed too late for our property, and I was never able to get anything.
The 800 and the 1,000, I got that, I think, in-- wait a minute.
I-- you couldn't get the money out at first, the 800 pounds.
You had to go to Germany to spend it, and you were allowed to withdraw so-and-so much per day.
So I-- I remember we went, I was already married.
So I must have had the money round around about '49-ish.
And we went to Germany, and I stayed the minimum amount of days to draw the money out.
I think it was perhaps 1950.
You got it-- the 800 pounds, you got in two lots.
The first lot, you had to go there.
The second lot, you were able to get in England.
So I went to get this, just for the reason of getting the money.
I think we went to-- we had to go to Frankfurt to get it.
I-- I just couldn't face Germany at that time.
And I wouldn't even speak to anybody.
I-- I made my husband, he could speak German.
Because he was born in Vienna.
He had to translate.
I wouldn't speak to anybody, in a shop or anything.
I-- I just wanted the money.
And I wanted to get out, and that's what we did.
We stayed there just the minimum of days to collect the 400 pounds.
And we came back to England.
Not at that time.
I don't think I was able to face it.
I did go much later.
I'll tell you what I remember.
We were in Frankfurt, and I did visit-- I wanted to visit the house where I was the, last address and the school.
And I remember asking somebody, do you know where [INAUDIBLE] is?
And they said, oh, you mean the Jewish district, in a most unpleasant way.
And I think that's why I wouldn't speak to anybody else after that.
That was my-- that's my impression that I remember.
That was probably-- must have been in very early '50s.
That's all I can remember from it.
Oh, yes.
I could speak-- by that time, I'd learned German again.
Because when I came back to London after the war, the hostel I was, in the B'nai B'rith hostel, they all could speak German.
But I speak a very childish German.
I mean, I can't-- I can't cope with a book or a newspaper.
I read too slowly.
I forget what I've read, you know.
Well, mainly English, but there was a mixture.
And-- and also when I was married, my first mother-in-law, her English was very poor.
And she spoke a mixture of German, English, and Yiddish.
So that's where I started to learn my Yiddish.
Because German Jews don't speak Yiddish.
Polish, Russian do, but not-- not German-- not if they're German Jews for several generations.
They didn't speak Yiddish.
And we never spoke Yiddish at home.
All-- all refugees, I would say, nearly all.
Our social network was very limited.
Because we were very hard up.
And we were also saving as hard as possible to try and buy a house.
So we didn't go out to grand places.
We went to the cinema once a week probably.
And we'd go to each other's house, and have a spaghetti, things like that, to each other-- when I say house, I mean to each other's [LAUGHS] rooms.
Very few of us had flats or houses then.
That came later.
That was basically our social life.
And we all worked, and I used to bring-- I used to work at home and I used to go out to work and work at home as well in the evenings.
Well, my first work was I worked in haute couture, which is upper-class dressmaking.
And you can starve to death on the wages you earn.
I then changed, because somebody offered me a little bit more money, to make hats.
And I became-- it takes a long time.
But I became a fully qualified milliner.
Because I earned a little-- only because I earned a little more money doing it.
My-- my ultimate work experience was when I married my-- well, I stopped-- I'm sorry.
I-- I then stopped work.
Because I had a child.
But I then started to work at home, because we needed the extra money.
And I worked together with a friend of mine as outdoor workers for a-- a very good class dressmaking firm.
And eventually when I married again, I worked in my husband's business, which was different again.
But in between marriages, I also used to, apart from working at home, I used to do-- I'm not religious by the way, anymore at all-- I took a Saturday job.
Because my ex-husband took care of the child on Saturdays, and I was able to work.
So I took a Saturday job in an antique shop, which I loved.
And gradually I did-- I did an extra day or two during the week as well, when I was able to.
Because, again, in those days you didn't-- you didn't put your children into nurseries.
Because you couldn't afford it.
You know, it would have swallowed up all the money you earned.
Susan was born in 1957.
I remember saying to my husband, when you earn 20 pounds a week, I shall stop work and have a baby.
[LAUGHS] And that's what happened.
When he earned 20 pounds a week, I stopped and had a baby.
Well, just, I mean you couldn't live like a king on that.
But we could just manage.
And then also after a couple of years, started to do work at home, I used to do.
I mean, I can make dresses and so on.
I used to do alterations and so on.
No.
I couldn't afford such luxuries.
But I did say, I'm not religious anymore.
I'm fiercely Jewish, but I'm not religious.
Why not?
Why not?

I think, you know, I have found that people who have gone through similar circumstances to me, they've either become even more religious or completely un-religious And I think-- I think what-- what finished-- finished it off completely was the experience I had because of my sister.
I think that finished it.
You'll have to excuse me for a minute.
[CRYING] I was afraid this would happen.
I hope I'm not on camera now.
My sister became an optician.
She studied in Edinburgh.
And unfortunately, she met a West Indian Indian who was studying to be a doctor.
He was already a solicitor.
His parents wanted him to be a solicitor.
And he was, apparently-- I mean I don't know how much of it is even true anymore.
Because I think he was a liar.
He was a solicitor.
But he wanted to be a doctor.
So he was studying to become a doctor, and they met in Edinburgh.
And against everybody's wishes and advice, she went back to Trinidad with him, when they both got their degrees, and married him.
I never knew the-- the details.
I only learned them later.
He was not a good man.
He used her mentally, physically, her money.
He was struck off as a doctor.
Because in those days, abortions were illegal.
I believe that's what he did-- he was caught doing an abortion.
And he was struck off.
And he more or less lived off her.
And I know that she wanted to leave him.
I found out this later, as I said.
And eventually I heard she went to America, where there is-- where I have a cousin, the only cousin that escaped concentration camp.
Did I say that before?
I may have done.
There was one cousin that survived concentration camp out of my-- of both sides of my family.
He lives in New York.
And there was other-- apparently, some very distant relatives there.
And there was a very kind woman that she lived with, who knew-- I think, knew the family, my-- my father's family.
And she'd set up everything there.
She had a-- she was-- she had a job.
She had a home to go to.
And they begged her to stay and not go back.
But she said, I have unfinished business, and I must go back to Trinidad, finish my business, and I'm coming back.
Unfortunately when she went back, he murdered her.
And he was subsequently hanged for it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I think that finished me off completely, my religion.
I-- I could face that my parents and everybody died in camp.
But this I-- I couldn't cope with.
How did I find out?
In fact, it took three months before I knew she was dead.
I had a letter from somebody, sending-- sending me condolences.
He had destroyed everything.
There was nothing there.
I mean, he knew me.
I knew him.
I knew his sister.
And I think I knew a brother.
But he had destroyed absolutely everything.
There were no records.
The police knew there was a sister somewhere in London.
I don't know how they knew this.
Oh yes, I do.
She worked-- before she worked for herself as an optician, she worked for an optical company in Trinidad, in Port of Spain.
And for some reason or other, she must have given my address to one of the-- I don't know if she was the owner or the-- somebody working there.
And this woman wrote to me to send me condolences.
And I had no idea she was dead.
And I thought-- I thought somebody was being funny.
I couldn't believe it.
And I remember spending the entire Saturday morning phoning every official body in London, from the home office to the foreign office and so on, trying to get this corroborated, if this were true.
Eventually I managed to get somebody who said to me, I will see what I can do for you.
And they phoned me back the next day, and told me they had had reports of it.
And from then on I had lots of reports, what was going on.
I tried to get help from the Jewish authorities.
And they didn't want to know, because she married out.
They wouldn't help me in any way whatsoever.
They wouldn't even find anything out.
Because we wanted to have her buried properly.
I was very hard up at the time.
I couldn't go there, because I didn't have the money.
And eventually the people were-- who really helped me were the St.
John's Ambulance Brigade.
Because I'd read in one report-- because I used to get papers and letters and things-- that she was an officer in the St.
John's Ambulance Brigade.
And I approached the St John's Ambulance Brigade in London.

And they took the trouble to see me, the head of the brigade saw me in London, and interviewed me, and they helped me an awful lot, and so did the head of the police in Trinidad, who was an Englishman, and came home on leave and he took the trouble to come and see me, although he lived in Cornwall.
And I asked him to try and find a few things of hers which were from Germany still.
They'd all disappeared.
There was nothing, nothing.
He had destroyed or sold or got rid of everything.
And eventually, when they did-- it was a very unpleasant case, the way the whole-- the-- the murder was a very unpleasant murder.
And it took the headlines for weeks and weeks.
It did appear in the British press once or twice.
And eventually my cousin, my older cousin that I've mentioned before, we put a gravestone up, which of course is still there and is being looked after.
And he was hanged for it.
Because in those days there's still-- it was a British colony still, and he was hanged sometime after the case was finished, or immediately after, I don't know which.
Completely.
They wouldn't help me at all.
They just didn't want to know.
I had to go to strangers to help me, and even the gravestone, I had to get somebody to-- to write the Jewish wording, the Hebrew wording, I should say, for me.
And I believe there is a Jewish community.
Perhaps I should have approached the Jewish community in Trinidad.
I believe there is a small community there.
I don't know.
And I've never been there.
Although I've been nearby to another island, I haven't had the guts to go to [LAUGHS] Trinidad.
Perhaps one day I'll have the guts to go there.
I don't know.
But I have no desire really to go.
No, we didn't belong to a synagogue.
Yes, we belonged-- I belonged-- we belonged to the B'nai B'rith Youth Group.
I think that disbanded though in about the '50s, the one that I belonged to.
Although they still meet once a year.
That particular crowd still meets once a year.
And I try and go to it whenever I can.
But I mixed in only Jewish circles.
And a lot of them with you know, refugee backgrounds like me, and still today a lot of them.
No.
I wouldn't leave England.
No.
No.
I wouldn't like to.
I suppose I should say I ought to go to Israel.
But I don't think I could take the climate anymore.
And I wouldn't, no.
There was a time when we were considering, my first husband.
We had papers to go to America, but we didn't.
And I don't want to live anywhere else now.
Because I think I owe lot to England.
No, I don't want to live anywhere else, I don't think.
I have no reason to.
If I had relatives, you know, close relatives perhaps, I would then think about it.
But there's no point.
I don't want to go-- I'm-- I'm too old now to start.
I don't feel I want to start all over again.
Well in the '50s, we did consider going to America, as I said.
But we decided not to.
I would say 50%.
I'm still in touch with some of them.

In fact, this coming weekend there's going to be a big party, where one of the girls from the hostel is going to be 70, and quite a few are coming to that, from Israel, from America, from London, from Wales.
We'll all be meeting at her house.
She lives in the country.
I think eight of us will be there, which is quite a number out of 15.
Oh, yes.
Well, out of the two hostels, there's a couple in-- there's a couple in-- there's two or three in Israel I am occasionally in touch with.
There's certainly two in London, three in London, and there's one outside London.
And there's one arriving tomorrow from America.
And she'll be staying here in this house with me that I was in the first children's home with.
So, I mean it's not a regular thing.
But we do sort of keep in touch.
She understands quite a bit of German.
And she understands French.
She speaks French, because of her father, who speaks perfect French, having been in France all during the war.
I have-- I have found this quite a difficult thing.
She really didn't want to know.
Whether it's a kind of a-- I can't explain it.
She used to say to me, oh Mommy, you should forget all that.
It's so long ago.
And I said, I will-- it's something one must never forget.
It's-- it's my background.
How can you forget your background?
And it's only in the last few years she's started to ask me questions, and wants to know things.
But she's a bit of a rebel, I think, my daughter.
Perhaps-- perhaps it's my fault, I don't know.
Perhaps it's because I was divorced.
But I've always tried to-- I've always tried to not antagonize her in any kind of way against her father, in fact, the opposite.
And she even said to me once, thank you for doing that.
Well, I-- I feel she ought to know about her grandparents, where we come from, what we are, what we did, what happened to them.
What she doesn't know, of course, and she knows about my sister, but nothing further than that.
Because what else can I add?
I can only tell her what-- I can only tell of what I think is fact.
You know, I don't want to make up stories.
I think I've given you a pretty sort of picture-- well, except that I married again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I now live in a very nice home.
We have-- my husband has a big extended family, although I have practically none.
Apart from that, I think I've given you a pretty comprehensive story about my background.
